Correctly describes GPU instancing requirements and shared mesh constraint
Identifies key limitations of each approach

questions.question: In a networked 3D game, explain client-side prediction and server reconciliation. Describe what happens when the server corrects a client's predicted position and how the correction is applied without visible teleporting.
questions.answer: Client-side prediction: The local client applies its own input to its character immediately, simulating movement without waiting for server confirmation. This hides the round-trip latency (e.g., 100ms), making movement feel responsive. The client stores a buffer of recent inputs with timestamps. Server reconciliation: The server simulates all clients authoritatively. When the server sends a state update for the local player, the client compares the server's position to where the client predicted it would be at that server tick. If they differ (due to missed collision or server-side correction), the client must correct. Correction without teleporting: The client rewinds its state to the server's authoritative state at the server tick, then replays all client inputs that occurred after that tick using the same simulation logic. This produces a corrected present-state position that accounts for the server's correction while preserving the client's recent inputs. The replayed result is usually very close to the current predicted position, so the correction is invisible or manifests as a tiny smooth adjustment rather than a teleport.

rubric.key: Correctly explains client-side prediction with input buffering
Describes server reconciliation with rewind and replay mechanism
Explains why correction is not visible as teleporting

questions.question: Describe how Wave Function Collapse works for procedural level generation. Include the initialization state, the constraint propagation process, and how contradictions are handled.
questions.answer: Initialization: A grid of cells is created, each containing the full set of possible tiles (maximum entropy). Adjacency rules define which tiles can be placed next to each other on each side (derived from example layouts or manually authored). Step 1 - Observation: Select the cell with the lowest entropy (fewest remaining possible tiles), breaking ties randomly. Collapse it to a single tile chosen from its possibilities (weighted by frequency). Step 2 - Propagation: Remove any tile options from neighboring cells that violate adjacency constraints with the newly placed tile. This removal may cascade: if a neighbor loses options, its neighbors must be rechecked. Continue until no more removals are possible. Step 3 - Repeat: Return to step 1 until all cells are collapsed. Contradiction handling: If propagation reduces any cell to zero possible tiles, a contradiction has occurred (no valid tile can go there). Options: (1) backtrack to the last observation and try a different tile, (2) restart generation with a different seed, or (3) place a special 'error' tile and continue (acceptable for some applications).

rubric.key: Correctly describes initialization with full possibility sets and adjacency rules
Explains observation (lowest entropy selection) and propagation cascade
Describes contradiction detection and at least one resolution strategy

questions.question: Explain what Addressable Assets are in Unity and describe two specific benefits they provide over direct asset references for a game targeting multiple platforms.
questions.answer: Addressable Assets are Unity's system for referencing assets by string address rather than direct object reference. Assets are organized into groups that define how they are packed and where they are loaded from (local or remote). Benefit 1: Content size management. Addressables let you split content into a base install and downloadable groups. A mobile game can ship a 100MB base app that meets store size limits, then download additional levels, characters, or textures on demand. Direct references force all referenced assets into the build regardless of whether they are needed immediately. Benefit 2: Post-launch content updates. Remote addressable groups can be updated on a CDN without submitting a new app build. This enables hotfixing art bugs, adding seasonal content, or A/B testing different assets by changing which remote bundle the game downloads. Direct references require a full app update through the store review process for any content change.

rubric.key: Correctly defines Addressable Assets with address-based reference system
First benefit is specific and clearly explained
Second benefit is specific and clearly explained

major_assignments.title: 3D Scene Construction and Interactive Environment
major_assignments.description: Create a fully interactive 3D environment with a playable character, multiple materials and lighting, physics-based interactions, and a functional camera system. The environment must contain at least 20 unique objects with appropriate colliders, a character controller with ground detection and jumping, a third-person camera with collision avoidance, and mixed lighting with at least 3 baked light sources and 2 realtime lights. Must maintain 60fps on target hardware.
objectives: Demonstrate proficiency with 3D scene composition and object placement
Implement a responsive character controller with physics-based interactions
Configure PBR materials with proper texture maps for all visible surfaces
Design a lighting setup that establishes mood while maintaining performance
deliverables: Unity project files with complete scene and all scripts
Windows build executable demonstrating all functionality
Performance profiling report with frame time breakdown (2 pages)
Technical documentation describing architecture decisions (3-4 pages)

criteria: Character controller handles ground detection, slopes, steps, and jumping correctly
Camera system provides smooth orbit control with wall collision avoidance
Physics interactions are stable with no tunneling, jitter, or exploding objects
Code is well-structured with clear separation of concerns and meaningful comments
PBR materials are applied correctly with albedo, normal, metallic, roughness, and AO maps
Lighting creates atmosphere with proper baked GI and realtime shadows
Visual theme is coherent and professionally presented
No visible artifacts: light leaks, Z-fighting, UV seams, or texture stretching
Maintains 60fps on mid-range hardware (documented with profiler data)
Draw calls are minimized through batching and instancing
Texture memory is managed with appropriate compression and resolution
Physics performance is reasonable with appropriate collider complexity
Technical decisions are explained with rationale and alternatives considered
Performance profiling data is presented with analysis of each subsystem
Challenges encountered and solutions applied are documented
Writing is clear, professional, and properly formatted
A polished, playable 3D environment with responsive controls
Visually cohesive lighting and materials that create atmosphere
Solid performance backed by profiling evidence
Thorough documentation demonstrating technical understanding

major_assignments.title: Advanced Rendering and Visual Effects Showcase
major_assignments.description: Build a visually impressive 3D scene that demonstrates mastery of advanced rendering techniques. The scene must include at least one custom HLSL shader (not Shader Graph), a terrain with splatmap texturing and vegetation, a complete post-processing stack, and at least one VFX Graph particle effect. The scene should tell a visual story through lighting and atmosphere.
objectives: Write a custom HLSL shader that implements a visual effect beyond standard PBR
Build and texture a terrain with procedural and painted detail layers
Design a post-processing stack that enhances the visual narrative
Create particle effects that integrate naturally with the environment
deliverables: Unity project with all custom shaders, terrain, and VFX assets
Windows build showcasing the complete visual experience
Shader code with extensive comments explaining each section
Technical art document describing visual pipeline and artistic intent (3-4 pages)
Before/after comparison images showing each technique's contribution

criteria: Custom HLSL shader compiles, runs correctly, and implements a non-trivial visual effect
Terrain splatmap texturing blends naturally with appropriate detail and normal maps
VFX Graph particle system demonstrates understanding of GPU particle simulation
Post-processing volume is configured with appropriate effect settings for the scene
The scene has a clear visual identity and atmospheric mood
Custom shader effect enhances rather than distracts from the scene
Lighting, post-processing, and effects work together cohesively
Terrain texturing appears natural without visible tiling or hard blending edges
Scene maintains 60fps with all effects enabled
Shader instruction count is reasonable for the target platform
Particle count is managed with LOD and distance-based emission
Terrain LOD is configured for efficient distant rendering
Shader code is thoroughly commented with mathematical explanations where applicable
Technical art document explains the visual pipeline from concept to implementation
Before/after comparisons clearly demonstrate each technique's impact
Writing is professional and suitable for a technical art portfolio
A visually stunning scene demonstrating advanced rendering mastery
Custom shader code that shows understanding beyond node-based tools
Cohesive visual design where all techniques serve the artistic vision
Portfolio-quality documentation and presentation

major_assignments.title: Networked 3D Multiplayer Prototype
major_assignments.description: Build a two-player networked 3D game prototype demonstrating client-server architecture, state synchronization, and latency compensation. The prototype must support two players connecting over a network, synchronize player positions with interpolation for remote players, implement client-side prediction for local player movement, and handle player connection and disconnection gracefully.
objectives: Implement client-server networking using Unity Netcode for GameObjects
Synchronize game state with proper authority and ownership models
Apply client-side prediction and entity interpolation for smooth networked movement
Handle network edge cases: connection loss, late join, and high latency
deliverables: Unity project with networking code and two-player support
Two builds or editor + build testing setup for multiplayer testing
Network architecture document explaining authority model and sync strategy (2-3 pages)
Video demonstrating gameplay with simulated 200ms latency

criteria: Players can connect, play, and disconnect without crashes
Server-authoritative state prevents basic cheating
Client-side prediction provides responsive local movement
Remote player interpolation is smooth with no teleporting
Players are visually distinguishable
Network state is reflected visually (connection status, player names)
No visual artifacts from network synchronization
UI communicates connection state clearly
Bandwidth usage is reasonable with delta compression
Gameplay remains playable at 200ms simulated latency
No frame rate impact from network processing
Graceful degradation under poor network conditions
Network architecture is clearly documented with diagrams
Authority and ownership model is explained with rationale
Latency compensation approach is described with before/after evidence
Known limitations are documented honestly
Functional networked multiplayer with smooth synchronization
Responsive gameplay despite network latency
Robust handling of connection edge cases
Clear documentation of networking architecture

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes assessing theoretical knowledge of 3D engine architecture, rendering, physics, optimization, and networking

lab_assignments.description: Weekly hands-on lab exercises in shader programming, physics setup, camera implementation, terrain building, and performance profiling

major_assignments.description: Three major projects: interactive 3D environment, advanced rendering showcase, and networked multiplayer prototype

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of 3D mathematics, rendering pipeline, materials, physics, and character controller concepts from weeks 1-7

final_project.description: Complete 3D game integrating all course concepts: rendering, physics, character control, camera, lighting, optimization, and deployment

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions and project presentations. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a reduced final grade. Missing a presentation without prior arrangement results in a zero for that component.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement. All builds must compile and run without errors on the target platform.
course_policies.collaboration: Individual assignments must be completed independently. Students may discuss concepts and debugging strategies but all code, assets, and documentation must be original work. Third-party assets must be properly attributed.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be original or properly attributed. Use of AI code generation tools must be disclosed and the student must be able to explain every line of generated code. Submitting unmodified Asset Store or tutorial projects as original work will result in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have access to a computer with a dedicated GPU capable of running Unity 2023.2 LTS. Minimum: 8GB RAM, GTX 1060 or equivalent. Lab computers are available but personal machines are recommended for out-of-class work.
